flashcards.front: Why is Host OS security critical in cloud computing?
flashcards.back: The Host OS has the highest privilege level after the hypervisor. A compromised Host OS allows an attacker to control all tenant VMs on that physical server, access virtual disks, and read memory — making it the foundation of cloud security.

flashcards.front: What are the key Host OS hardening strategies?
flashcards.back: 1) Minimal installation (only essential packages). 2) Dedicated purpose (only hypervisor + management). 3) MAC enforcement (SELinux/AppArmor). 4) File integrity monitoring (AIDE/Tripwire). 5) Secure Boot + TPM. 6) Isolated management network. 7) Automated patch management.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between DAC and MAC access control?
flashcards.back: DAC (Discretionary): Traditional Unix permissions where owners control access to their resources — flexible but prone to misconfiguration. MAC (Mandatory): System-enforced policies that override DAC — SELinux and AppArmor implement MAC. MAC is preferred in high-security cloud environments.

flashcards.front: What OS vulnerabilities are unique to cloud environments?
flashcards.back: 1) Shared kernel attacks in containers (kernel exploit affects ALL containers). 2) Management OS (Dom0) exposure — compromising it equals compromising the hypervisor. 3) Side-channel attacks (Spectre/Meltdown) that leak data across VM boundaries through shared hardware.

flashcards.front: What is immutable infrastructure and how does it improve OS security?
flashcards.back: Instead of patching live VMs, deploy new VMs from updated golden images and destroy old ones. This ensures consistent, known-good configurations and eliminates configuration drift. Modern cloud-native approach to Guest OS patching.

flashcards.front: What are namespaces and cgroups in Linux OS security?
flashcards.back: Namespaces isolate process groups (PID, network, mount, user) — they are the foundation of container isolation. Cgroups control resource allocation (CPU, memory, I/O) per process group, preventing resource exhaustion attacks. Together they provide OS-level virtualization.
